articles.title: UK to rejoin Erasmus+ in 2027
articles.summary: The UK will rejoin the Erasmus+ mobility programme in 2027 for an initial one-year term, ending its six-year absence that began after Brexit in 2021. Erasmus+ is the European Union’s flagship programme supporting education, training, youth, and sport across Europe.
articles.insight:
The UK’s planned 2027 re-entry into Erasmus+ may modestly improve its appeal to European students seeking mobility opportunities, though broader international demand—especially from Asia and the Americas—remains more influenced by post-study work rights, tuition value, and visa stability than Erasmus participation alone.  
Admissions teams should track uptake of UK-EU student exchanges post-2027, monitor whether partner institutions in Europe formalise new bilateral agreements, and assess if this development meaningfully shifts application patterns among priority EU source markets before adjusting recruitment messaging or resource allocation.

articles.title: Canada overhauls immigration consultant regulations
articles.summary: The Canadian government has introduced new regulations for immigration consultants that include increased penalties for misconduct, an expanded public register of authorized consultants, and a compensation mechanism for victims of dishonest practices. These changes aim to enhance oversight and protect individuals seeking immigration services.
articles.insight:
Canada’s stricter immigration consultant rules may indirectly influence international student demand by reinforcing perceptions of regulatory rigour and service reliability, though direct impacts on enrolment decisions depend on how prospective students interpret these changes relative to broader study-abroad considerations.  
Admissions teams should monitor shifts in Canadian application volumes from key source markets—especially India and Nigeria—as well as student sentiment around immigration pathway transparency, while reviewing their own pre-departure support offerings for alignment with evolving global regulatory expectations.

articles.title: India seeks database of outbound students amid global conflicts
articles.summary: India’s Ministry of External Affairs is planning to create a dedicated database platform for Indian citizens traveling abroad for education and employment. This initiative aims to enhance emergency coordination in response to ongoing global conflicts, including the Russia-Ukraine war and recent Middle East tensions.
articles.insight:
India’s move to track outbound students reflects growing government concern over safety in volatile regions, potentially reinforcing student and family preferences for stable, English-speaking destinations like the USA, UK, Australia, and New Zealand—though direct demand shifts remain uncertain without accompanying policy or funding changes.  
Admissions teams should monitor India’s database rollout timeline and any linked advisories or support mechanisms, as these may influence pre-departure decision-making, agent partnerships, and institutional crisis-response messaging for Indian applicants.

articles.title: Global degree, lower cost: Why 2+2 Programmes are winning over Indian families
articles.summary: The article explains that 2+2 programmes—where students complete the first two years of a degree in India and the final two years abroad—are gaining popularity among Indian families due to their lower overall cost compared to full four-year overseas degrees. These programmes offer a globally recognized degree while significantly reducing tuition, accommodation, and living expenses.
articles.insight:
The growing appeal of 2+2 programmes in India signals a potential shift toward cost-conscious, hybrid pathway preferences—though it remains unclear whether this reflects declining appetite for full overseas degrees or simply expanded strategic options amid persistent demand for global credentials.  
Admissions teams should monitor uptake of articulation agreements with Indian institutions, track conversion rates from 2+2 to full-degree enrolment, and assess whether partner colleges’ academic rigour and student support align with their brand and quality standards.

articles.title: Australia speeds up immigration review for highly skilled workers
articles.summary: Australia has announced it will expedite immigration processing for highly skilled foreign workers. The government will allocate 70% of the 2026 permanent migration quota to this group.
articles.insight:
Australia’s accelerated immigration pathway for highly skilled workers may indirectly boost its appeal to international students—particularly those from regions with strong post-study work aspirations—though direct demand shifts depend on how effectively universities communicate these opportunities and whether students perceive long-term residency as attainable.  
Admissions teams should track enrolment trends among students targeting skilled migration pathways, monitor policy implementation timelines, and assess whether partner agents or source markets are increasingly framing Australian study as a strategic step toward permanent residence.

articles.title: Universities urged to focus on “factors they can control” as policy settings depress international student enrolments in the Big Four
articles.summary: Restrictive immigration policies in Australia, Canada, the UK, and the US reduced new international student enrolments at universities in those countries during the first quarter of 2026. These policies also increased student interest in Asian and European destinations, as reported in the most recent Global Enrolment data.
articles.insight:
Restrictive immigration policies in the Big Four nations appear to be dampening new international enrolments, potentially reflecting shifting student demand toward more welcoming destinations—though causality remains complex given concurrent economic, geopolitical, and institutional factors.  
Admissions teams should monitor real-time application trends by region and visa refusal rates, while assessing whether partner agents and students signal growing preference for alternative study destinations amid evolving policy sentiment.

articles.title: US to end “Duration of Status” for F, J, and I visas and limit the time international students can study in the US
articles.summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security proposed in August 2025 to eliminate “Duration of Status” for F, J, and I visas, replacing it with fixed time limits for international students. Under the proposal, most international students would be required to complete their programs by September 2026—or within four years of entry—unless granted an extension by U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services.
articles.insight:
This proposed U.S. policy shift—replacing open-ended “Duration of Status” with fixed four-year limits for F/J/I visa holders—may dampen U.S. appeal for students from regions where longer or flexible study pathways are valued, though actual enrolment impact hinges on final rule details and implementation timing.  
Admissions teams should track the rule’s progression through federal review, assess how peer institutions in the UK, Australia, and NZ position themselves on visa certainty, and begin scenario-planning for potential shifts in student inquiries toward destinations offering more predictable post-study timelines.

articles.title: Ho Chi Minh City teachers ordered to assess their English skills as Vietnam eyes second-language plan
articles.summary: Ho Chi Minh City has directed all teachers—except those who teach English—to self-assess their English proficiency by May 12. This initiative supports Vietnam’s national goal of establishing English as a second language in schools by 2035.
articles.insight:
This policy signals Vietnam’s intensified national commitment to English proficiency, which may gradually increase demand for high-quality English-medium education abroad—though direct impacts on short-term enrolment remain uncertain given domestic upskilling efforts.  
Admissions teams should monitor Vietnamese student application trends by English test scores and destination preferences over the next 12–18 months, while reviewing scholarship messaging that aligns with evolving local perceptions of English-language value.
